sentences.text: Well, when we boarded the train and left Pittsburgh, our first stop was Atlanta, Georgia.
And they had prepared lunch for us.
So it was a warm day.
And we had some of the windows open.
So this one white fellow walking by, and he said, why are you stopping here?
He asked the conductor.
And the conductor said, oh, to feed that bunch of nigger soldiers back there.
So the officer that was on the train, [INAUDIBLE] fellows, just ignore it and remain calm.
Beause we were getting ready to-- Because what?
Because we were getting ready to get off of that train and give him a shellacking.
So we just ignored it.
And he told us that the officer-- he was a white officer.
He told us, you're going to encounter a lot of unpleasant remarks now that you're in the South.
He said, you have to realize that.
Yes.
Right, black soldiers.
Yes.
No.
Well, they had situations there, when we were at Camp Forest in the-- see, after that, they switched us from airbase security to quarter master.
That take us down to the railhead.
To the railhead, they call it.
It was an expression that they used.
It's like depot, the railroad.
They'd take us, load us on trucks, take us down there.
And we'd work unloading these box cars and things.
Then they had a-- they was shipping food and stuff in for all the whole base.
Then they had a big bakery there.
And they had baked this bread.
And then I would get a loaf of this hot bread.
And get me a pound of butter and just smear-- just buttering this hot bread and eat it, make a meal off of it.
And then we'd work.
They'd take us back to camp and we'd eat in our area.
And then at that time, there were a lot of-- you'd pass these white women.
And one day, [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] the authority came and says, you have to stop that group, bunch of nigger soldiers, from eyeball raping these women.
He called it eyeball rape.
So eyeball raping these white women, that's the way he put it.
So you can imagine the officers.
They said, they have eyes.
If women don't want to be seen, you have to remove the women off the camp, off of this base.
So I just took it lightly, didn't pay any attention to it.
Just ignored it.
I said, these people are ignorant.
And to me, I said ignorance is bliss.
How did you respond to other type of verbal words thrown at you?
Well, I never allowed it to annoy me.
Now, if they want to be ignorant, let them be ignorant.
Because they're ignorant.
I'm not going to be ignorant.
We shipped out of Camp Forrest at-- let's see.
That would have been March of '44.
March of '44, was set for Camp Shanks, New York.
We shipped over to Europe.
By train.
About two weeks.
Well, I took a bus down to Philadelphia with a fellow soldier.
Because I had not gotten the furlough.
I was the only solider in the outfit that didn't get a furlough.
Because I was in the hospital that time there.
Take 15 day furloughs.
Yes.
Well, I was having trouble with my eyes.
And the doctors were trying to find out just what was causing the difficulty.
You see, when I--
Yes, vision.
Yes.
My vision was 70 over 16 in one eye and 60 over 40 in the other eye, because when I went into the military, I wanted to get in the Marine Corps.
And my vision-- the Marines turned me down and said my vision was too poor for a Marine.
Let's see.
That would have been two weeks after we arrived at Camp Shanks, New York.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] at first we were scheduled to go on the Queen Mary.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then they changed the orders, and we went off on the Liberty Ship.
SS McAndrew.
McAndrew.
Liberty Ship.
We went over on a Liberty Ship and returned on a Liberty Ship.
To Liverpool, England.
Oh, it took us 12 days. 12 days to go over, and 14 days coming back.
Well, they boarded us on trains and took us to a town [INAUDIBLE] called Upton Scudamore.
Upton Scudamore.
It was a town in England.
And we continued in basic training there.
Then they took us to Frome.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
No.
In England, it was different.
Well, the people were much nicer.
They knew that we had come over to assist in defeating the Nazis.
Well, when we would get passes and go into town, the different towns, it was-- we were received very warmly.
The different pubs, you'd go into town and go to the pub.
And you had fish and chips.
They have fish and chips and a cup of tea for meals.
You know, new meals.
They are big fish and chip eaters over there.
When you get them.
I was in England [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] about three or four weeks before I crossed the English Channel.
And--
Yes.
And the white soldiers, they took a lot of their prejudice over there.
And they would tell the Englishman that we were monkeys.
And that they'd go out at night, said we had a tail.
And we would be swaying through the trees with our tail.
And so one incident, this-- he was a corporal, and I had gone to town and passed.
And this English girl ask him to see his tail.
Until he says, you want to see my tail?
And he unbuckled his belt and dropped his pants.
Plus his undergarment.
Then he said, now you see my tail?
She says, no, I don't see no tail.
He says, well you asked to see my tail, that's the only tail I got.
So the white MPs, they arrested him and brought him back to camp.
And we had a colonel there in charge of that outfit.
He told me, he said you take this soldier back to town.
And he told the black soldier.
He says, now, if they ask to see your tail again, you do the same exact thing.
Show them your tail.
Because they had spread-- the white soldiers had spread all sorts of rumors about us to the Englishmen.
Some of them were unintelligent enough to believe these things.
But you take some intelligent people, a lot of intelligent people in England, and they didn't believe it.
They took it with a grain of salt.
They just ignored it.
But those are some of the things I encountered in England.
Yes.
Southampton.
That's right on the Channel, the English Channel.
And as far as you could see, there was nothing but soldiers.
Soldiers getting ready to cross the English Channel into France.
Oh, I don't know.
You could look out across the channel and see the coast of France.
Yeah.
Yes.
On an LST.
That was these boats that they would have-- small boats that they would have, where they'd put so many tanks and trucks and soldiers on it.
And whenever it hits the beach, they'd lower the front end of it.
And the soldiers would mark that-- walk the ramp.
Run the ramp onto the beach.
And you would stand in Southampton, the English side of the channel, and see those LSTs.
When they'd lower that door, the Germans had that 88.
It's a big canon.
And they would fire the 88, and you'd see Jeeps and bodies flying in the air, and full colonels and majors and all these supposed to be macho men crying like babies.
And they had MPs, they were sub-Thompson machine guns making them go up by their boats.
Because you shouldn't cry in the face of the enemy.
You're supposed to be shot by your fellow soldiers.
And they would make them go up.
I didn't cry.
I got on them.
Oh, yes.
They went to Omaha Beach, across the channel.
Well, it was-- well, I'll just say it was hell.
It was hell.
Well, it was raining.
I don't remember the exact day.
That would have been '44.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Oh, wait a minute.
That was June the 8th.
June 8th, 1944.
It was two days after D-Day.
D-Day was the 6th of June.
Well, shells and bullets were flying everywhere.
Not there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But as we advanced, some were lost.
Oh, we landed.
And it was a downpour.
They had buckets of water falling out of the sky.
Then see, they gave us these uniforms that was supposed to have been impregnated.
And they-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in spite of the pregnation of the uniform, I was drenched to my skin.
And before we got to the bivouac area where we were going to bivouac that night, we had to march 15 miles inland.
And when we got to this bivouac area, you couldn't strike a match or nothing at night for fear that a German plane would spot it and bomb the area.
Or come down and strafe the area, whichever.
And-- so I just took my tent shelter half--
My-- see, they put two soldiers together in each squad.
And one carried half of the tent shelter, and the other one carried the other half.
And when they reach a bivouac area, they'll take the two halves and put them together.
So I just took my half and rolled up in it on the ground.
And I was soaked to my skin.
And then, the next morning, we got up and built a big bonfire.
Tried to dry our clothing out.
And I dried my clothing out.
And there was-- oh, that was my new-- the conditions under which I had to sleep.
As we advanced, I slept on the ground where it was snow.
Or tried to sleep.
You know, just rest.
In the snow, rain, whatever the weather conditions were.
No.
That's as we advanced.
Well, the first-- see, our outfit was attached.
We were called-- referred to as Combat Services Supply.
And then we went in-- we were attached to the different divisions.
When we went in on Omaha Beach, we were attached to the Rangers.
The Rangers were made up of hardened criminals.
And if they lived through it, they'd go and establish these beach heads, and if they lived through it, they got their freedom.
But they would only allow them to establish beach heads.
They wouldn't allow them to go into the cities and towns, because they were bank robbers, safe crackers, and everything else in these Rangers.
And they were supposed to be hardened like a Marine.
Killers, stone killers.
From the prisoners here in the United States-- the Alcatraz's and all those prisons.
That's what they were.
And so then after that, when they pulled up out, we were attached to the first division.
When we went in the first French town [INAUDIBLE].
Saint-Lo.
Saint-Lo.
S-T-L-O.
Saint-Lo.
That was the first French town to fall.
And we went in, but the first invasion pushed us out.
Germany pushed us back out.
Then while the 1st Division was regrouping, they sent us in with the 29th Infantry Division.
The 29th Infantry Division went in and re-took Saint-Lo and held it.
And after they had retook Saint-Lo, there was one side or one building standing right there.
Everything else was rubble.
Then--
We went up from Saint-Lo to [INAUDIBLE].
All those towns in southern France.
Normandy.
Yes.
And I had read about Normandy in the geography books and so forth.
And I had the opportunity to see it and see how those people lived.
And then I--
Well, they treated us all right.
It was difficult.
But you take a lot of people in Normandy, even to this day, dislike what the Americans did, because we had tear up towns.
You know, routing those Nazis out of there.
And they didn't like us for tearing up their towns.
Because at least their towns were intact when the Germans occupied them.
You got some of the older people that are still living there, had lived through it, still resent what we did.
But we had to do it.
We had no-- no other alternative but to do it.
Well, we headed to Paris.
Sainte-Genevieve-des-Bois, many towns, little small towns and hamlets that we went and crossed through.
[INAUDIBLE] army.
I was an officer's orderly.
Because my eyes, they see the commanding officer told me my eyes was too weak, too bad to be out there on the battlefield.
And so most of the time I spent around guarding the command post with the soldiers that were at camp, once the other soldiers went out and worked.
